coredata.dc:title: Novel g–C3N4–carbon dots-aggregation/ferrite hybrid heterojunction photocatalyst with excellent visible-light-driven photodegradation performance toward organic pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Jing
Hui, Shengjie
Lin, Xuemei
Xie, Jie
Wang, Qi
Li, Yuchun
Ying, Zongrong
coredata.dc:description: In water pollution treatment, photocatalytic technology is attracting intensive attention and photocatalysts have extensively been focused on, but high efficiency still remains expected. Herein, a spherical uniformly-dispersed aggregation photocatalyst of carbon dots (CDs) with g-C3N4 was hydrothermally synthetized through employing glucose as carbon sources for CDs and employing cyanuric chloride and dicyandiamine for the formation of g-C3N4, and then coupling with 10 wt% of new hydrothermal ferrite photocatalyst Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 to fabricate a novel g–C3N4–CDs-aggregation/ferrite hybrid heterojunction photocatalyst through post sintering process. Various techniques including SEM, HRTEM, XRD, XPS, UV–vis and EIS spectroscopy were employed to analyze the as-fabricated ternary hybrid photocatalyst. Due to the uniformly-dispersed modification of CDs to g-C3N4 and the hybrid heterojunction between g–C3N4–CDs aggregation with Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4, the targeted hybrid photocatalyst presented excellent visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation efficiency towards methylene blue, with a high degradation ratio of up to 96.6% in 1 h, corresponding to a photodegradation rate constant k value of up to 0.05127 min−1. Our targeted hybrid photocatalyst shows good structural and photocatalytic stability and electronic radical (e−) is the main active species during photocatalytic process.

head.ce:title: Novel g–C3N4–carbon dots-aggregation/ferrite hybrid heterojunction photocatalyst with excellent visible-light-driven photodegradation performance toward organic pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Material Science and Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, 210094, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Material Science and Engineering
Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Material Science and Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, 210094, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In water pollution treatment, photocatalytic technology is attracting intensive attention and photocatalysts have extensively been focused on, but high efficiency still remains expected. Herein, a spherical uniformly-dispersed aggregation photocatalyst of carbon dots (CDs) with g-C3N4 was hydrothermally synthetized through employing glucose as carbon sources for CDs and employing cyanuric chloride and dicyandiamine for the formation of g-C3N4, and then coupling with 10 wt% of new hydrothermal ferrite photocatalyst Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 to fabricate a novel g–C3N4–CDs-aggregation/ferrite hybrid heterojunction photocatalyst through post sintering process. Various techniques including SEM, HRTEM, XRD, XPS, UV–vis and EIS spectroscopy were employed to analyze the as-fabricated ternary hybrid photocatalyst. Due to the uniformly-dispersed modification of CDs to g-C3N4 and the hybrid heterojunction between g–C3N4–CDs aggregation with Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4, the targeted hybrid photocatalyst presented excellent visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation efficiency towards methylene blue, with a high degradation ratio of up to 96.6% in 1 h, corresponding to a photodegradation rate constant k value of up to 0.05127 min−1. Our targeted hybrid photocatalyst shows good structural and photocatalytic stability and electronic radical (e−) is the main active species during photocatalytic process.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing environmental pollution, people have paid intense attention on environmental protection, especially on water pollution treatment [1–3]. In the field of water pollution removal, photocatalytic technology is attracting intensive attention from both industry and academia and one of the most expected green environmental protection strategies [4–6]. Photocatalysts are the key of photocatalytic technology, which have extensively been focused on recently [7–12]. Among them, graphite-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has stood out due to its prominent advantages such as cheap sources, no metal elements and moderate band gap [13,14]. However, pure g-C3N4 suffers from two major shortcomings, i.e., low visible-light absorption rate and high photo-generated carrier recombination rate, which result in its low-performing in environmental pollutant removal under visible light irradiation [15–19]. Because of this, in recent years, various modification methods have been employed to improve its visible light activity, for example, doping other atoms like metallic or nonmetallic elements [1,20–22], coupling with other photocatalysts or some conducting polymer [23–27].

Carbon dots (CDs), a novel eco-friendly zero-dimensional nanocarbon material, has attracted considerable attention in photocatalytic field because of their unique optical and electronic properties [28–30]. CDs possesses excellent electron transfer ability, thus can reduce the recombination of photogenerated electron/hole pairs in photocatalytic process. Additionally, CDs has superior up-conversion fluorescent properties, thus can expand the optical absorption range [31,32]. Many researches have demonstrated that when semiconductor photocatalysts such as TiO2 [33], ZnO [34,35], Ag3PO4 [36] or g-C3N4 [37,38] combine with some CDs, they will absorb a longer wavelength visible light and then emit high-energetic light to generate more charge carriers, thus improving the photocatalytic performance.

In recent years, ferrite has drawn much interest from photocatalytic researchers. Ferrite has broad light absorption range of 200–800 nm with high absorption intensity, and thereby possesses excellent photoelectric conversion efficiency under visible light. And more, ferrite is easily and conveniently collected with the help of external magnetic field for recycled use because of its unique magnetic properties [39,40]. However, pure ferrite has relatively short lifetime of photo-generated carriers due to their easy recombination inside the crystal [41], thus suffering from low photocatalytic degradation performance. Through combining with other photocatalysts like ZnO [42] and with carbon materials like graphene [43], fabricating ferrite hybrid photocatalysts is a feasible avenue for improving photocatalytic efficiency [44]. Among them, p-type ferrite coupling with n-type g-C3N4 to form p-n heterojunction can promote the transfer of photo-induced electrons [45,46], thus some g-C3N4/ferrite hybrid photocatalysts are reported to exhibit a certain level of improvement in photocatalytic performance [47–51].

The previous-reported g–C3N4–based binary or ternary heterostructured photocatalysts commonly adopted nanometer flake g-C3N4, which was prepared through direct thermal sintering followed by ultrasonical or thermal exfoliation. Commonly, CDs was hydrothermally synthetized on the surface of semiconductor photocatalysts to achieve the purpose of modification. Herein, employing glucose as a carbon source for CDs and employing cyanuric chloride and dicyandiamine for the formation of g-C3N4, we for the first time hydrothermally co-synthetized a spherical uniformly-dispersed aggregation photocatalyst of CDs with g-C3N4 (CNCDs), and further employing low mass dosage of novel hydrothermal photocatalyst Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 to couple with CNCDs, followed by post sintering process with aims to form heterojunction, a novel CNCDs/ferrite hybrid heterojunction photocatalyst was successfully fabricated. We found that CNCDs50 greatly expanded the optical absorption range of g-C3N4 and meanwhile enhanced the optical absorption strength, and that the heterojunction between CNCDs50 with Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 exhibited outstanding contribution to photocatalytic performance. Compared with two similar g-C3N4/CDs/Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 ternary hybrid heterojunction photocatalysts (employing hydrothermal monomer photocatalysts g-C3N4 and Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4), which are fabricated through post-sintering the g-C3N4/Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 physical mixture hydrothermally anchored CDs on or through post-sintering the sintered g-C3N4/Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 hybrid heterojunction hydrothermally anchored CDs on, we also found that our targeted CNCDs/Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 hybrid heterojunction photocatalyst possessed better photocatalytic performance. Due to the uniformly-dispersed modification of CDs to g-C3N4 and the hybrid heterojunction between g–C3N4–CDs complex and Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4, our targeted hybrid photocatalyst presented excellent visible-light photocatalytic degradation efficiency towards methylene blue (MB), superior to many recently-reported photocatalysts in MB removal [14–16,19,20,23–26,28,38,48–50], suggesting a promising application in pollutant removal from environment.

Acetonitrile (CH3CN), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O) were purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dicyandiamide (C2H4N4), 30% hydrogen peroxide aqueous solution (H2O2), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), silver nitrate (AgNO3), isopropanol (IPA), disodium edetate (EDTA-2Na) and p-benzoquinone (BQ) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O) was supplied by Xiqiao Chemical Co., Ltd. Cyanuric chloride (C3Cl3N3), glucose (C6H12O6) and MB (C16H18ClN3S) were purchased from Tianjin Institute of Chemical Reagents. All the reagents were analytically pure and used as received without further purification. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Preparation of CNCDsX: 1.844 g (0.01 mol) of cyanuric chloride and an appropriate amount of glucose were added into 40 mL of acetonitrile solution. After magnetically stirring, 0.617 g (0.007 mol) of dicyandiamine was added. After mechanically mixed for 12 h, the mixture was placed in a 100-mL hydrothermal kettle and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. Then, it was centrifuged and washed with water to obtain carbon-dot-modified carbon nitride, which was referred to as CNCDsX (X represents the added mass of glucose in mg). CNCDs10, CNCDs30, CNCDs50 and CNCDs70 corresponds to 10, 30, 50 and 70 mg of glucose addition, respectively.

Synthesis of g-C3N4: 1.844 g (0.01 mol) of cyanuric chloride was added into 40 mL of acetonitrile and stirred at room temperature for 0.5 h. And then 0.617 g (0.007 mol) of dicyandiamide was added. The solution was mechanically mixed for 12 h and heated at 180 °C for 24 h in a 100-mL hydrothermal kettle. The product was washed several times with water and dried overnight in an oven at 70 °C to obtain a pale yellow powder, i.e., g-C3N4 (abbreviated as CN).

Synthesis of Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4: 0.0238 g (0.1 mmol) NiCl2·6H2O, 0.186 g (0.9 mmol) CoCl2·6H2O, 0.54 g (2 mmol) FeCl3·6H2O and 1 g citric acid (for optimizing ferrite structure) were added into 30 mL of deionized water and magnetically stirred to obtain a homogeneous solution. Then, 6 mol L−1 NaOH was slowly added dropwise to make the pH of the solution reach 11. Under the condition, the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 2 h and then placed in a hydrothermal kettle at 180 °C for 48 h. The black precipitate was centrifuged, washed several times to make the pH of the solution reach 7 and dried overnight in an oven at 70 °C. The as-obtained solid material Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 (abbreviated as NCF) was ground into a powder with a grinding crucible.

Preparation of ternary physically-mixed photocatalyst MNCF-CNCDs50: The CNCDs50 obtained according to 2.2.1(2) and a corresponding mass of NCF (the mass ratio of CNCDs50:NCF is 9:1) were dispersed in 60 mL of deionized water by mechanically stirring for 1 h and then the dispersion was dried at 70 °C until the water was completely evaporated. Finally, the mixed photocatalyst MNCF-CNCDs50 was obtained.

Preparation of ternary sintered hybrid heterojunction photocatalyst MSNCF-CNCDs50: Powdery MNCF-CNCDs50 sample was placed in a porcelain boat, and sintered at 500 °C (reached from room temperature at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1) for 2 h in a nitrogen atmosphere to obtain MSNCF-CNCDs50.

Preparation of ternary physically-mixed photocatalyst MNCF/CN-CDs50: The CN obtained in 2.2.1(1) and a corresponding mass of NCF (the mass ratio of CN:NCF is 9:1) were added into 60 mL of ethanol, followed by mechanical stirring and ultrasonication for 40 min, respectively. The dispersion was dried at 70 °C until its ethanol is completely evaporated to obtain a powder mixture. Next, the powder mixture was sintered at 500 °C for 2 h in a nitrogen atmosphere and naturally cooled to room temperature to obtain NCF/CN. Subsequently, the as-obtained NCF/CN is dispersed in aqueous solution containing 50 mg of glucose by mechanically stirring for 1 h and then placed into a 100-mL hydrothermal kettle to be heated at 180 °C for 5 h, followed by suction filtration and drying at 70 °C overnight, and finally a powdery production MNCF/CN-CDs50 was obtained.

Preparation of ternary sintered hybrid heterojunction photocatalyst MSNCF/CN-CDs50: Powdery MNCF/CN-CDs50 sample was placed in a porcelain boat and sintered at 500 °C for 2 h in a nitrogen atmosphere, followed by naturally cooling to room temperature to obtain MSNCF/CN-CDs50.

Preparation of ternary physically-mixed photocatalyst MNCF-CN-CDs50: The CN obtained in 2.2.1(1) and a corresponding mass of NCF (the mass ratio of CN:NCF is 9:1) is dispersed in aqueous solution containing 50 mg of glucose by mechanically stirring for 1 h and then placed into a 100-mL hydrothermal kettle to be heated at 180 °C for 5 h, followed by suction filtration and drying at 70 °C overnight, and finally a powdery product MNCF-CN-CDs50 was obtained.

Preparation of ternary sintered hybrid heterojunction photocatalyst MSNCF-CN-CDs50: Powdery MNCF-CN-CDs50 powder sample was placed in a porcelain boat and sintered at 500 °C for 2 h in a nitrogen atmosphere, followed by naturally cooling to room temperature to obtain MSNCF-CN-CDs50.

ce:section.ce:para: The crystal structure was determined by a German Druhan D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation (0.15416 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was taken on an American Thermo Fisher Scientific Escalab 250 with a monochromatic Al Kα as X-ray source to obtain the elemental composition. The morphology was observed by a Japanese Hitachi S-4800 field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) and a Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN High resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM). The nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms were measured by a Micrometrics ASAP 2020 analyzer at 77 K, and based on the BJH method, the pore size distribution analysis was performed from the desorption isotherm. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) was measured with Thermo Fisher Scientific's EV 220 UV–Vis spectrometer. UV-6100S UV spectrophotometer (Shanghai Mepuda Instrument Co., Ltd.) was employed to evaluate the absorbance of MB. The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals were detected with Bruker A300 at the ambient temperature.

Under visible light irradiation, we carried out photo-catalytic degradation of MB in aqueous solution to investigate the photo-catalytic performance of the as-obtained photocatalysts. The specific steps are as follows: A 300 W xenon arc lamp was used as the light source (the filter in the Xenon arc lamp equipment was used to filter out UV emissions below 420 nm), and 25 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in 50 mL of 10-mg L−1 MB aqueous solution for investigation. To ensure adsorption-desorption equilibrium, the suspension was magnetically stirred in the black for 30 min. Then, 0.8 mL of 30% H2O2 solution was added. The temperature of the reaction chamber was controlled at 25 °C by continuous circulation water. And then the light source was turned on for producing visible light to irradiate the above solution. After every interval of 10 min, 4 mL of the solution was taken out. After centrifugation, the light absorbance of the taken solution was measured at λ = 664 nm (the characteristic peak location of MB) by an ultraviolet spectrophotometer to ascertain the MB concentration within the taken solution. For better comparison, in addition to the difference of catalysts, the comparative experiments was performed under the same conditions. As for MB solution, the photocatalytic degradation process follows the pseudo-first-order equation as:
(1)

where k is the degradation rate constant, C
0 and C
t represent the concentration of MB at the initial stage of irradiation and after irradiation for t time, respectively.

Based on the magnetic of our targeted composite photocatalyst, during the cycling stability test, the MSNCF-CNCDs50 catalyst was recovered through using a magnet attached outside the test tube, and then washed by water several times to remove MB adsorbed by the catalyst. After dried, the recovered MSNCF-CNCDs50 catalyst was used for the next cycling test.

In active species induction test, the scavengers used were AgNO3, EDTA-2Na, BQ and IPA, whose concentrations were 1 mmol L−1. Active species electron (e−), hole (h+), superoxide radical (•O2 −) and hydroxyl radical (•OH) were detected. Prior to opening the light source, the scavengers were added to the quartz tube, except which the photodegradation operation procedure was the same as mentioned above.

Employing XRD technique, three monomer photocatalysts CNCDs50, CN and NCF as well as six as-prepared composite photocatalysts based on g-C3N4, CDs and Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 were determined to understand their crystal structure. Fig. S1 shows the XRD patterns of the as-synthetized CN, CNCDs50 and NCF after being sintered at 500 °C for 2 h in a nitrogen atmosphere. Both CN and CNCDs50 exhibit an obvious diffraction peak at 27.5°, which is the typical inter-layer stacking (002) plane characteristic peak of g-C3N4 (JCPDS card No. 87-1526) [30], demonstrating that both CN and CNCDs50 are g-C3N4 material. For CNCDs50, two weak peaks around 24° and 44° are also observed, which are the characteristic peaks of CDs, suggesting the existence of CDs. NCF shows a characteristic peak at around 35.6°, which corresponds to the (311) surface of Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4, but it exhibits a low angular shift compared with the XRD standard pattern of CoFe2O4 shown in the JCPDS database (No. 22-1086) [40] because of the addition of 10% Ni2+ into pure CoFe2O4. The XRD patterns of six as-prepared hybrid photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 1 . For the three ternary physically-mixed photocatalysts, there all is an obvious diffraction peak at 27.5° (Fig. 1(a)), proving that the they all contain g-C3N4 component. However, as can be seen from Fig. 1(b), their corresponding sintered hybrid photocatalysts present a relatively weak peak at 27.5°, demonstrating that during sintering process the hybridization of ferrite partially do some damage to the graphitization degree of g-C3N4 and some hybrid heterojunction was formed. For all the six composite photocatalysts, a characteristic peak located around 35.6° can be observed in their XRD patterns, and meanwhile all composite photocatalysts present two peaks around 24° and 44°, meaning the existence of CDs.

To further confirm the chemical composition and state, the XPS spectrums of our targeted hybrid photocatalyst MSNCF-CNCDs50 were determined and are displayed in Fig. 2 . The XPS full spectrum of Fig. 2(a) suggests the co-existence of six elements C, N, O, Fe, Co and Ni. Of course, because the dosage of ferrite added was very low, the peaks of Fe, Co and Ni are very weak. As far as C1s is concerned, we can know from its spectrums (see Fig. 2(b)) that C element mainly exists in C–C (285.0 eV) and N–CN (287.5 eV) bonds [20]. According to the N1s spectrums in Fig. 2(c), the characteristic peaks at 401.0, 399.2 and 398.5 eV are attributed to C–N–H, N-(C)3 and C–NC, respectively [20]. These prove the existence of g-C3N4 inside the MSNCF-CNCDs50 photocatalyst. For the O1s spectrums in Fig. 2(d), the characteristic peak at 532.7 eV reveals the presence of O–H bond while one at 531.7 eV is generated from the Fe–O bond [52]. In the Fe2p spectrum (Fig. 2(e)), there are five sub-peaks. 727.5 eV represents the 2p1/2 binding energy of Fe2+ and Fe3+, the peak at 723.7 eV is attributed to 2p1/2 of Fe2+. The characteristic peak for 2p3/2 of Fe2+ is located at 713.0 eV while the peak for 2p3/2 of Fe3+ is centered at 710.4 eV. The peak at 717.0 eV is an ancillary peak of the above four peaks. These peaks reveal the coexistence of Fe3+ and Fe2+ in MSNCF-CNCDs photocatalyt [45]. According to Fig. 2(f), Co2p mainly shows two characteristic peaks at 796.4 eV (Co2p1/2) and 781.3 eV (Co2p3/2) [40]. Regarding Ni2p, as shown in Fig. 2(g), the hybrid peaks of Ni2p1/2 and Ni2p3/2 are located at 872.5 and 854.9 eV, respectively, while the peaks around 862.4 and 856.4 eV correspond to N2p1/2 and Ni2p3/2, respectively [49]. From the above analysis, it can be concluded that MSNCF-CNCDs50 is a hybrid photocatalyst containing g-C3N4 and a low content of Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4.

Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of CNCDs50 and three sintered ternary hybrid photocatalysts MSNCF-CN-CDs50, MSNCF/CN-CDs50 and MSNCF-CNCDs50. According to Fig. 3(a), the as-prepared CNCDs50 binary hybrid photocatalyst are spherical with different sizes of about 100 nm~1 μm in diameter. MSNCF-CN-CDs50 presents severely-damaged spherical morphology and many particles are very irregular, which results from the physicochemical reaction between monomers during the sintering process, as can be seen from Fig. 3(b). In Fig. 3(c) and (d), it can be found that MSNCF-CNCDs50 and MSNCF/CN-CDs50 show similar appearance, composed of numerous subglobular particles clustered.

Additionally, the specific surface area and pore size distribution of MSNCF-CNCDs50 photocatalyst are investigated employing nitrogen adsorption-desorption measurements, as comparatively displayed with that of CNCDs50 and NCF in Fig. S2. From Fig. S2, we can see that there nearly is no existence of micropores for three samples. The specific surface areas of CNCDs50, NCF and MSNCF-CNCDs50 are 0.519, 36.9 and 9.86 m2 g−1, respectively. This reveals that NCF is much finer particles than CNCDs50, which is obviously compatible with the SEM observation in Fig. 3(a) and (d). According this, we can roughly distinguish CNCDs50 and NCF particles in Fig. 3(d). Of course, due to too small in size, CDs can't be seen in SEM images.

In order to clearly discriminate g-C3N4, Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 and CDs inside our targeted photocatalyst, we employed the TEM and HRTEM techniques to observe CNCDs50 and MSNCF-CNCDs50 and the corresponding images are presented in Fig. 4 . From Fig. 4(a), we can see that CNCDs50 is sphere with a cloud-like surface. In Fig. 4(b ~ c), many CDs grains can be distinctly seen and the CDs grains is relative uniform in size, i.e., about 2 nm in size. Specially, in Fig. 4(c), we can obviously find that the surfaces of most CDs grains are covered by the regular crystal fingers of g-C3N4, which reveals that the CDs grains evenly distributes inside the g-C3N4 sphere instead of only on its surface, i.e., due to the co-formation of g-C3N4 and CDs during the hydrothermal process, CNCDs50 is an integrative complex of g-C3N4 and CDs. From Fig. 4(d ~ f), some subglobular particles are seen clustered together, which is in good concordance with the SEM observation from Fig. 3(d). In Fig. 4(g ~ h), we can't see the CDs grains seen in Fig. 4(c), but we can see the crystal lattice attributed to Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4, based on which we can confirm the existence of CNCDs50 and Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 inside our targeted photocatalyst MSNCF-CNCDs50, as marked in Fig. 4(d ~ f). Of course, inside the marked CNCDs50 in Fig. 4(d), we can't see CDs, which is easy to understand because CDs is only seen in the thin edge of CNCDs50, while in Fig. 4(c), the thin edge region is Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 and CNCDs50 is in the thick central region.

Further, the elemental distribution and content of MSNCF-CNCDs50 photocatalyst was analyzed and shown in Fig. 3. As seen from Fig. 3(a–g), MSNCF-CNCDs50 photocatalyst contains C, N, O, Fe, Co and Ni elements, which are evenly distributed within the whole photocatalyst. It could be found from Fig. 5 (h) that the contents of three elements C, N, and O are relatively high while those of Fe, Ni and Co are very low, which is consistent with their applied content, respectively. Specially, the molar ratio of C to N is higher than that of C3N4, indicating that the addition of glucose indeed leads to the formation of some CDs during hydrothermal process.

Based on the above XRD, XPS, SEM and TEM analysis, we can conclude that our targeted photocatalyst MSNCF-CNCDs50 is composed of g-C3N4, Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 and CDs, and is a hybrid photocatalyst of the uniformly-dispersed g–C3N4–CDs aggregation with Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4, which are connected together by heterojunction between them.

The UV–vis diffuse reflection tests were performed on CN, CNCDs50 and six ternary hybrid photocatalysts, and the obtained UV–vis data are converted by Kubelka-Munk formula to obtain their corresponding band gap spectra, displayed in Fig. 6 . From Fig. 6(a), because of the modification of CDs formed from glucose during hydrothermal process, CNCDs50 presents much broader light absorption range than CN, which is due to superior up-conversion fluorescent properties of CDs [31,32], confirming the existence of CDs. According to Fig. 6, the order in absorption intensity is MNCF/CN-CDs50 > MSNCF-CNCDs50 > CNCDs50 > MSNCF-CN-CDs50 > MNCF-CNCDs50 > MNCF-CN-CDs50 > MSNCF/CN-CDs50. For all of them, compared with pure g-C3N4, both the overall absorption intensity and light absorption range increase in varying degrees. In Fig. 6(b), the intersection between the tangent of the graph and the abscissa is the band gap value of photocatalysts. CN shows a band gap energy of approximately 2.70 eV and the other photocatalysts present a band gap between 1.0 and 2.0 eV. Among them, compared to CNCDs50, the band gap of MNCF-CNCDs50 photocatalysts is larger than CNCDs50 while those of other ternary photocatalysts are less than CNCDs50.

EIS test is one of the effective methods for detecting photocatalysts. Generally, based on the arc radius of the EIS spectra, the magnitude of their impedance value can be calculated. The smaller the arc radius is, the smaller the impedance value is. The smaller impedance reveals lower electron transport resistance and higher electron separation efficiency, which is beneficial to the photocatalytic degradation. It can be seen from Fig. 7 that compared with CNCDs50, three ternary hybrid heterojunction photocatalysts MSNCF-CN-CDs50, MSNCF/CN-CDs50 and MSNCF-CNCDs50 obtained after sintering show smaller arc radius, revealing that the formation of some hybrid heterojunction between CN, CDs and NCF leads to lower electron transport resistance, which corresponds to higher electron separation efficiency. However, three physically-mixed photocatalysts MNCF-CN-CDs50, MNCF/CN-CDs50 and MNCF-CNCDs50 present higher electron transport resistance than CNCDs50, which is understandable because the physically-mixed photocatalysts possess high interfacial resistance between unheterojuncted g-C3N4, CDs and Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4. Therefore, it can be known that MSNCF-CN-CDs50, MSNCF/CN-CDs50 and MSNCF-CNCDs50 have higher electronic separation efficiency than MNCF-CN-CDs50, MNCF/CN-CDs50 and MNCF-CNCDs50. Among them, MSNCF-CNCDs50 has the smallest arc radius, i.e., the highest electron separation efficiency. Based on this, we can roughly infer that MSNCF-CNCDs50 should possess the best photocatalytic effect, which will be proved by the subsequent investigation in photocatalytic performance.

To investigate the photocatalytic performance of the as-obtained composite photocatalysts, MB was used as a probe to perform photocatalytic degradation under visible light. Fig. 8 (a) shows the effect of the CDs content on MB degradation for CNCDsX. With the increase of the amount of glucose added, the degradation effect first increases and then decreases. When the addition mass of glucose reaches 50 mg, the photocatalytic performance of CNCDs50 is the best. According to Fig. 8(b), among three physically-mixed photocatalysts, MNCF/CN-CDs50 shows the best photocatalytic degradation effect with photocatalytic degradation ratio of 80% in 1 h. This should be because that the NCF/CN component inside MNCF/CN-CDs50 was formed via sintering the mixture of NCF and CN, so necessarily there were some heterojunction formed in the NCF-CN interface inside MNCF/CN-CDs50, while MNCF-CNCDs50 and MNCF-CN-CDs50 were two completely simple physical mixtures, thus there was no existence of heterojunction inside them.

Obviously, three sintered hybrid photocatalysts MSNCF-CNCDs50, MSNCF-CN-CDs50 and MSNCF/CN-CDs50 present relatively stronger photocatalytic performance than their corresponding physically-mixed photocatalysts MNCF-CNCDs50, MNCF-CN-CDs50 and MNCF/CN-CDs50, respectively. This reveals that during the sintering process, there is new microstructure formed, i.e., there is the emergence of some hybrid heterojunction in the interface between CN and NCF. Among three sintered hybrid photocatalysts, MSNCF-CN-CDs50 presents the best photodegradation performance, demonstrating that hydrothermally co-synthetized uniformly-dispersed CN-CDs aggregation possesses the best improved effect on photocatalytic efficiency. Inside MSNCF-CN-CDs50 and MSNCF/CN-CDs50, CDs was anchored on the surface of CN, NCF or NCF/CN, thus probably CDs enriching on the surface partially shielded the absorption of CN or NCF to excitation light. As far as MSNCF-CN-CDs50 is concerned, some CDs likely embedded inside the hybrid heterojunction between CN and NCF, while its worse photodegradation performance compared to MSNCF/CN-CDs50 seems to indicate that CDs being embedded inside the hybrid heterojunction is unfavourable to photocatalytic performance. Our targeted photocatalyst MSNCF-CNCDs50 almost completely degraded MB within 1 h, superior to many recently-reported photocatalysts in MB removal performance [14–16,19,20,23–26,28,38,48–50].

Especially interesting, compared to their corresponding physically-mixed photocatalysts, among three sintered hybrid photocatalysts, the sintering bring very small improvement in photocatalytic efficiency for MSNCF/CN-CDs50, while the improvement is medium for MSNCF-CN-CDs50 and most prominent for MSNCF-CNCDs50. The reason for this is understandable as following: the post-sintering only resulted in little new heterojunction for MSNCF/CN-CDs50, while for MSNCF-CN-CDs50, the post-sintering can lead to many NCF-CN heterojunction but there are some CDs embedded within these heterojunction, and for MSNCF-CNCDs50, the heterojunction resulted from the post-sintering also is about as many as MSNCF-CN-CDs50 but these emerging heterojunction is between NCF and uniformly-dispersed-CDs-modified CN-based aggregation with much broad light absorption range.

As shown in Fig. 8(c), the order in photocatalytic degradation performance is MSNCF-CNCDs50(96.6%) > MSNCF/CN-CDs50 (90.2%) >MNCF/CN-CDs50 (86.2%) > MSNCF-CN-CDs50(82.8%)>CNCDs50(58.5%)>MNCF-CN-CDs50(47.5%)>MNCF-CNCDs50(23.4%). The degradation reaction rate constant k values of MSNCF-CNCDs50, MSNCF-CN-CDs50, MSNCF/CN-CDs50 and CNCDs50 are 0.05127, 0.02827, 0.0356 and 0.01495 min−1, respectively. The k value of MSNCF-CNCDs50 is up to 3.43 times one of CNCDs50, demonstrating that the hybridization of NCF further greatly promotes the improved photocatalytic efficiency towards MB of CNCDs50 photocatalyst.

For photocatalysts, cycling stability is crucial for their recovery and reuse, and therefore is an important needed property in addition to photocatalytic efficiency. The cycling stability of the targeted MSNCF-CNCDs50 photocatalyst was assessed employing four cycles of photocatalytic degradation experiments towards MB. Fig. 9 (a) shows that in the fourth cycle, the degradation ratio of MB still is up to above 92%, indicating good photocatalytic cycling stability. With the existence of magnetic NCF, the recycling of hybrid photocatalyst is more convenient and faster, performed only with the help of magnet. The XRD analysis of MSNCF-CNCDs50 before and after four cycles of photocatalytic degradation experiments demonstrates that the crystal structure has no obvious change during photocatalytic degradation, as shown in Fig. 9(b), displaying good photocatalytic cycling stability.

The mechanism of MB degradation by MSNCF-CNCDs50 under visible-light irradiation was examined through investigating the reactive species of photocatalytic process with the help of four frequently-used scavengers BQ, EDTA-2Na, IPA and AgNO3 for active species •O2 −, h+, •OH and e−, respectively. As shown in Fig. 10 (a), when BQ, EDTA-2Na or IPA was added, the photocatalytic degradation process and effect of MB was not obviously affected. However, after adding AgNO3, the degradation process of MB was significantly hindered, and the degradation efficiency is only 1/5 of that without adding any scavengers, indicating that the addition of AgNO3 performs a significant inhibition to the photocatalytic degradation. In addition, we performed an ESR test on electron. From Fig. 10(b), it can be seen that the intensity of electron increases under visible light irradiation. Fig. 10 demonstrates that electrons play an important role in the photocatalytic degradation process, in other words, electrons are the main active species of the photocatalytic degradation reaction aroused from MSNCF-CNCDs50 photocatalyst.

The possible photocatalytic mechanism of our targeted hybrid heterojunction photocatalyst is illustrated in Fig. 11 . In MSNCF-CNCDs50, the CDs inside CNCDs50 plays three roles in the photocatalytic process [53,54]. First, due to its upconversion photoluminescence properties, CDs can help the g-C3N4 inside CNCDs50 to absorb much wider spectrum of visible light than pure g-C3N4, which excites CNCDs50 to form much more electron-hole pairs, thus there are much more electron contributed to the photocatalytic process. Second, the photogenerated electrons inside the g-C3N4 can be transferred to CDs, which improves the separation of electron-hole pairs and make more electrons for photocatalysis. Third, CDs acted as both electron acceptors and donors, which makes electrons easily transfer to the surface of CNCDs50 to perform the photocatalytic reactions, specially, makes many electrons transfer to the heterojunction interface between CNCDs50 and NCF, subsequently fast transfer to the CB of NCF. The electrons in the CB of NCF works with H2O2 to make MB photodegraded and meanwhile there are a few electrons in the CB of CNCDs50 to perform some photocatalysis.

Our hydrothermally synthetized CDs-modified g-C3N4 is a uniformly-dispersed aggregation of CDs with g-C3N4, which possesses spherical appearance and g-C3N4 chemical structure, with obviously smaller band gap energy, much broader light absorption range and lower electron transport resistance than pure g-C3N4. With the further hybridization of 10% Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 and the formation of some hybrid heterojunction resulted from post sintering, the as-obtained g–C3N4–CDs-aggregation/Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 hybrid photocatalyst showed obvious further enhanced visible-light absorption and obvious further improved electron separation efficiency. The degradation ratio is up to 96.6% in 1 h, corresponding to a photodegradation rate constant k values of up to 0.05127 min−1, up to 3.43 times that of CNCDs50. For various ternary hybrid photocatalysts based on g-C3N4, CDs and Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4, post sintering makes different improved contribution to photodegradation performance and shows the most prominent improved effect in our targeted g–C3N4–CDs-aggregation/Ni0.1Co0.9Fe2O4 hybrid photocatalyst. Our targeted hybrid photocatalyst possesses good structural and photocatalytic stability and the main active species is electronic radical (e−) during photocatalytic process.
